flashcards.Back: The muscular tube extending from the back of the nasal and oral cavities to the esophagus and trachea. It serves as a passageway for air and food.
The middle part of the pharynx located behind the mouth. It functions as a channel for both the digestive and respiratory systems.
The upper part of the pharynx located behind the nasal cavity. It provides a passageway for air during breathing.
A leaf-shaped structure that prevents food and liquid from entering the trachea during swallowing. It acts as a protective flap over the glottic opening.
The windpipe that connects the larynx to the bronchi. It allows air to pass to and from the lungs.
A firm cartilage ring forming the lower portion of the larynx. It provides structure and support to the airway.
The voice box located above the trachea. It contains the vocal cords and serves as an air passage.
The two major branches of the trachea that lead to the lungs. They subdivide into smaller air passages ending at the alveoli.
Tiny air sacs in the lungs where oxygen and carbon dioxide are exchanged. They are surrounded by a network of capillaries.
The primary muscle of breathing that separates the chest from the abdominal cavity. Its contraction causes inhalation, and its relaxation causes exhalation.
The active process of breathing air into the lungs. The diaphragm and intercostal muscles contract, increasing the size of the thoracic cavity.
The passive process of breathing air out of the lungs. The diaphragm and intercostal muscles relax, decreasing the size of the thoracic cavity.
The process where oxygen-rich air enters the alveoli and crosses into the blood capillaries. Simultaneously, carbon dioxide moves from capillaries into the alveoli.
The normal, adequate breathing rate for an adult is 12 to 20 breaths per minute.
The normal, adequate breathing rate for a child is 15 to 30 breaths per minute.
The normal, adequate breathing rate for an infant is 25 to 50 breaths per minute.
Breathing that is sufficient to support life. It is characterized by a normal rate, regular rhythm, and adequate chest expansion.
Breathing that is not sufficient to support life and requires artificial ventilation. Rates may be outside normal ranges with irregular rhythms or inadequate depth.
A blue-gray skin discoloration indicating inadequate oxygenation or poor perfusion. It is often seen in the lips, nail beds, and mucous membranes.
The pulling in of the muscles between the ribs, above the clavicles, or below the sternum. It indicates an increased effort of breathing, primarily seen in children.
Occasional, irregular, gasping breaths that may be seen just before death. They are a sign of inadequate breathing and require immediate artificial ventilation.
The widening of the nostrils during inhalation. It is a sign of respiratory distress, especially common in infants and children.
Infants and children have smaller airways that are more easily obstructed. Their tongues take up proportionally more space in the mouth than adults.
A method used to open the airway of a patient with no suspected neck or spinal injury. It involves tilting the head back while lifting the chin.
A method used to open the airway of a patient with a suspected spinal injury. The jaw is moved forward without tilting the head or extending the neck.
The process of using a vacuum device to remove blood, vomitus, and other liquids from the airway. It is essential to maintain a clear air passage.
Also known as a tonsil tip or Yankauer catheter. It is used to suction the mouth and oropharynx of an unresponsive patient.
A flexible catheter useful for suctioning the nasopharynx or when a rigid catheter cannot be used. It should only be inserted as far as the base of the tongue.
Suctioning should be performed for no more than 15 seconds at a time in an adult patient. This prevents prolonged hypoxia.
Suctioning should be performed for no more than 10 seconds at a time in a child patient.
Suctioning should be performed for no more than 5 seconds at a time in an infant patient.
A properly functioning portable or mounted suction unit should generate a vacuum of at least 300 mmHg when the tubing is clamped.
A method of artificial ventilation using a pocket mask with a one-way valve. It is preferred when used with supplemental oxygen at 15 liters per minute.
A handheld device with a face mask, self-inflating bag, and oxygen reservoir used to provide artificial ventilations. It should be connected to high-flow oxygen.
The most effective method of BVM ventilation. One rescuer maintains an airtight mask seal using two hands while the second rescuer squeezes the bag.
A single rescuer forms a C around the ventilation port with the thumb and index finger to hold the mask, using the other fingers to lift the jaw.
An adult patient with inadequate breathing should be artificially ventilated at a rate of 10 to 12 breaths per minute, or one breath every 5 seconds.
Infants and children should be artificially ventilated at a rate of 20 breaths per minute, or one breath every 3 seconds.
Flow-restricted, oxygen-powered ventilation device. It provides a peak flow rate of 100% oxygen at up to 40 liters per minute and is for use in adults only.
Artificial ventilation provided through a permanent surgical opening in the neck. A pediatric mask is often used to make a proper seal over the stoma.
A rigid curved device inserted through the mouth to keep the tongue from blocking the airway. It is used only in unresponsive patients without a gag reflex.
Measure from the corner of the patient's lips to the bottom of the earlobe or angle of the jaw to select the correct size.
A flexible tube inserted through the nose to maintain an open airway. It is less likely to stimulate a gag reflex and can be used in patients with altered mental status.
Measure from the tip of the nose to the tip of the earlobe to select the correct size. It must be lubricated with a water-soluble lubricant before insertion.
Seamless steel or aluminum cylinders that contain compressed oxygen. Common sizes used in prehospital care include D, E, and M cylinders.
Devices attached to oxygen cylinders that reduce the high internal pressure of the tank to a safe delivery pressure for the patient.
The preferred method of giving oxygen to prehospital patients. It can deliver up to 90% oxygen when the reservoir bag is kept full.
The flow rate should be adjusted to 15 liters per minute so that the reservoir bag does not completely collapse when the patient inhales.
An oxygen delivery device with two prongs inserted into the nostrils. It is used only when patients will not tolerate a non-rebreather mask.
The flow rate for a nasal cannula should be set between 1 and 6 liters per minute, delivering a lower concentration of oxygen.
